11-16.context: Our beliefs about the good life are tested against the choices we actually make. The gap between stated belief and lived choice is always worth examining.

11-17.prompt: What does the approach of winter ask you to turn toward?
11-17.context: Winter invites a kind of interior turning — less outward expression and more inward attention. What does this season ask you to look at more closely?

11-18.prompt: What is one way you could be more generous in the next few weeks?
11-18.context: The end of the year tends to invite generosity. Rather than a vague intention, what is the specific, concrete act of giving that would mean something to you and someone else?

11-19.prompt: What has your body been asking for that you've been too busy to hear?
11-19.context: Bodies communicate continuously, but we listen selectively. What has yours been signaling — in fatigue, tension, or hunger — that you've been too distracted or proud to respond to?

11-20.prompt: What is a word or phrase that has guided you this year, knowingly or otherwise?
11-20.context: Sometimes an idea organizes a year's choices without being explicitly named. Looking back, what word or idea has been at the center of how you've lived?

11-21.prompt: What are you most afraid of, and is that fear proportionate to the actual risk?
11-21.context: Fears are rarely examined directly — we navigate around them. Naming your deepest current fear and testing its proportionality is both courageous and clarifying.

11-22.prompt: What would it mean to finish this year with integrity?
11-22.context: Not perfection — integrity. Alignment between what you value and how you're living in the final weeks. What would that actually require of you?

11-23.prompt: What are you thankful for that you've never put into words?
11-23.context: Gratitude lives in us without always surfacing. There are things — people, conditions, small mercies — that we feel but rarely articulate. What is yours?

11-24.prompt: What is something you want to preserve from this year — a memory, a feeling, a practice?
11-24.context: Years contain things worth keeping. Being deliberate about what you want to carry forward — not just what to release — is also part of honest reflection.

11-25.prompt: Where in your life are you still waiting to be given permission that you could give yourself?
11-25.context: We wait for external validation or permission far more than we realize. Identifying one place where you're still waiting — and recognizing you have the authority to proceed — is freeing.

11-26.prompt: What does it mean to be genuinely present at a table with people you love?
11-26.context: Gathering at a table is one of the oldest rituals we have. What would it mean to be more fully there — less distracted, more grateful, more honest — in the conversations that matter?

11-27.prompt: What do you want to stop explaining or justifying about yourself?
11-27.context: We spend energy defending choices and traits that are simply ours. Naming one thing you're done apologizing for — and feeling settled in that — is a form of self-acceptance.

11-28.prompt: What is one thing you've received this year that you didn't deserve or earn?
11-28.context: Grace — in the secular sense of unearned gift — appears in every life if we look honestly. Naming what has come to you without being warranted is a form of genuine humility.

11-29.prompt: What does the coming winter ask you to protect?
11-29.context: Winter is a season of protecting essentials — warmth, light, connection. What in your inner life needs protecting as the cold and dark increase?

11-30.prompt: What do you want to carry into December, and what do you want to leave in November?
11-30.context: The final month of the year deserves intentional entry. Naming what you bring and what you release — even if the categories are small — is an act of self-awareness.

12-01.prompt: What do you want December to feel like, and what would make that possible?
12-01.context: December can be hectic or deliberate. Naming the quality you want to inhabit — and what would actually create it — gives you something to return to when the month pulls you away.

12-02.prompt: What is one thing this year has cost you, and was it worth the cost?
12-02.context: Every meaningful year costs something — energy, comfort, certainty, or the version of yourself you used to be. Asking honestly whether the cost was worth it is part of closing the year well.

12-03.prompt: What does the quiet of winter invite you to sit with?
12-03.context: Winter's reduction of light and pace is an invitation most of us don't accept. What becomes visible or audible when you slow down and stop filling the silence?

12-04.prompt: Who has shown up for you this year in ways that mattered?
12-04.context: The people who quietly showed up — without drama or announcement — deserve to be named. Gratitude that names specifically who and what is more meaningful than the general kind.

12-05.prompt: What is one thing you want to understand better about yourself before this year is done?
12-05.context: The end of the year is a good time to direct honest attention at yourself. What remains genuinely opaque about who you are or how you work?

12-06.prompt: What have you learned about love — in any form — that you want to carry forward?
12-06.context: A year is long enough to teach you something about love that you didn't know in January. What is the lesson, and what does it ask of you going forward?

12-07.prompt: What would it mean to end this year without regret about how you spent your time?
12-07.context: Regret is often about time — what we did with the days. With weeks remaining, is there time to adjust what you'd otherwise look back on with disappointment?

12-08.prompt: What does integrity mean when no one is watching, and do you practice it?
12-08.context: Character in private is the truest measure of character. What do you do — and not do — when there's no audience and no consequence but your own sense of yourself?

12-09.prompt: What is one story about yourself that you're ready to retire?
12-09.context: The stories we tell ourselves shape what we attempt and what we avoid. Some are well past their usefulness. What is one you'd like to stop using to explain or limit yourself?

12-10.prompt: What does hope feel like for you right now, and what is it directed toward?
12-10.context: Hope at year's end can be tired or renewed. Examining what you're currently hoping for — and whether the hope feels alive — is a useful measure of where you are.

12-11.prompt: What small thing has consistently brought you joy this year that you might not have noticed?
12-11.context: Large joys are easy to name. The small, recurring ones — a ritual, a view, a sound, a moment — are often the more reliable and underappreciated sources.

12-12.prompt: What is one thing you want to do in the final weeks of this year that would make it feel complete?
12-12.context: Completion is a feeling, not just a fact. What is the act, conversation, or moment that would give this year a sense of having been fully lived?

12-13.prompt: Where in your life have you been most true to yourself this year?
12-13.context: Authentic living requires navigation — moments of truth amid many invitations to perform or conform. Where this year did you most clearly act from your actual values?

12-14.prompt: What do you want the year ahead to be organized around?
12-14.context: Not a list of goals, but a central quality or commitment — a word, a theme, an intention that can organize choices when the path forward is unclear.

12-15.prompt: What is one habit from this year that has genuinely served you and deserves to continue?
12-15.context: Annual reflection often focuses on what to change. What is worth keeping? Naming a practice that has genuinely helped and committing to it consciously is equally important.

12-16.prompt: What has been the most valuable thing you've learned about yourself this year?
12-16.context: A year is a significant unit of time. Distilling the most important self-knowledge you've gained — even tentatively — is a worthwhile close to twelve months.

12-17.prompt: What relationship do you want to invest in more deeply next year?
12-17.context: Relationships take energy and intention. Rather than vaguely valuing them all, naming one specific relationship you want to deepen gives you a concrete place to begin.

12-18.prompt: What does it feel like to approach the year's end with honesty about how it actually went?
12-18.context: The temptation at year's end is to round up — to make the year more successful or more resolved than it was. What does the truly honest version look like?

12-19.prompt: What do you want to forgive — in yourself or in another — before the year closes?
12-19.context: Carrying resentment or self-blame into the new year is a choice, even if it feels inevitable. What is the forgiveness that is most ready to happen?

12-20.prompt: What does the increasing darkness of December ask you to sit with?
12-20.context: The year's darkest days carry a particular invitation to turn inward. What does the stillness surface for you when you stop filling it with noise and activity?

12-21.prompt: What does the solstice ask you to hold and to release?
12-21.context: The winter solstice is the year's deepest turning — the longest night before the light begins to return. What have you been carrying through the dark that is ready to be released?

12-22.prompt: What are three things this year has given you that you would not trade?
12-22.context: Not everything good about a year is comfortable or expected. Some of the most valuable things are hard. What has this year given you — regardless of form — that you wouldn't give back?

12-23.prompt: What does it mean to be generous with your spirit in the days to come?
12-23.context: Generosity of spirit — patience, warmth, presence — is needed most in busy, stressful gatherings. What would that actually look like in the specific situations ahead of you?

12-24.prompt: What is the gift you most want to give to the people you love, beyond anything material?
12-24.context: The most valuable things we can offer to those we love are rarely purchasable. What is it that they most need from you, and can you offer it more fully?

12-25.prompt: What does this day mean to you, separate from obligation or expectation?
12-25.context: However you mark this day, it carries meaning beneath the surface. What does it hold for you when you look at it honestly, beyond what it's supposed to be?

12-26.prompt: What do you want to do with the quiet days between Christmas and the new year?
12-26.context: The final stretch of the year has a particular, in-between quality. How do you want to inhabit it — with rest, reflection, connection, or something else?

12-27.prompt: What would you like to say to yourself that you haven't found the words for yet?
12-27.context: There is usually something we know we need to hear that we've been waiting for the right moment to say. The year is ending — this is a good moment.

12-28.prompt: What has the year asked of you that surprised you?
12-28.context: Years rarely unfold as expected. The unexpected demands — and how you met them — often reveal more about you than anything you planned for.

12-29.prompt: What is the most honest one-sentence description of how this year actually went?
12-29.context: Resist the urge to qualify or soften. What is the truest single sentence you could write about the year you've lived?

12-30.prompt: Who do you want to be when the new year begins?
12-30.context: Not a list of achievements but a quality of character — an intention for how you want to inhabit the days ahead. Who is the person you're beginning to become?

12-31.prompt: What are you most grateful for as this year closes, and what will you carry forward?
12-31.context: The last day of the year is an invitation to hold the whole of it — the hard parts and the good parts — with gratitude and honest attention. What will you take with you into what comes next?
